coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots with Lewis-Basic nitrogen sites for electrocatalyst CO2 reduction to CH4

coredata.dc:description: Metal-free carbon-based catalysts offered a promising alternative for efficient electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction (ECO2RR), presenting potential advantages over transition metal-based counterparts. However, challenges such as low Faraday efficiencies and limited current densities persist. Herein, we synthesized CQDs with high Lewis basic sites to enhance CO2 adsorption and activation, resulting in an optimized CQD-2 catalyst that achieved a Faradaic efficiency of 52.00 % and a current density of 178 mA cm−2 for CH4 production. In situ characterizations unveiled that CO2 was activated into *COOH intermediates, which further converted to *CO species. Concurrently, activated H2O molecule ionized into protons, facilitating reaction kinetics. This research provides valuable insights into designing carbon-based catalysts with high Lewis base content, offering significant prospects for advancing high-performance ECO2RR technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dioxide reduction
Lewis basic sites
Hydrophobic
Carbon quantum dots
Methane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of catalysts and structural characterization
Understanding the correlation between physicochemical properties and electrochemical performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and morphological characterization. (a) A schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CQDs. (b) TEM image and (c) Lateral size distribution of CQD-2. (d) HRTEM image of CQD-2. (e) AFM image and (f) Histogram of particle size distribution of CQD-2. (g) UV–vis absorption of CQD-2.

Structural characterization of different CQDs. (a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra, (c) High-resolution C 1 s spectrum and (d) High-resolution N 1 s spectrum of CQD-2. (e) N spectra analysis of CQDs with different nitrogen-containing functional group contents.

Electrochemical performance of different CQDs without IR compensation. (a) Schematic diagram of a flow cell for ECO2RR. (b) Faradaic yields of ECO2RR products of CQD-2 over different potentials. (c) Faradaic yields of ECO2RR products at −2.0 V vs RHE of all CQDs. (d) Linear sweep voltammetry curves of different CQDs. (e) Partial current density of CH4. (f) Tafel slopes of different CQDs. (g) Electrochemically active surface areas. (h) Zeta potentials. (i) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

Research on CQD-2 electrocatalyst reaction mechanism. In situ ATR-FTIR spectra recorded during ECO2RR at −0.2 V vs RHE from 0-20 min in (a) CO2-saturated and (b) Ar-saturated 0.5 M KHCO3 solution. (c) In situ Raman spectra recorded during ECO2RR at −0.4 V vs RHE from 0-20 min in 0.5 M KHCO3 solution. (d) The proposed reaction route of CH4 formation.
Heat maps indicating Pearson correlation for different physical and chemical properties of CQDs with (a) FE(CH4) at −2.0 V vs RHE; (b) -J(CH4) at −2.0 V vs RHE.

head.ce:title: Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots with Lewis-Basic nitrogen sites for electrocatalyst CO2 reduction to CH4

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By synthesizing CQDs with Lewis basic sites, activating surface water molecules on the catalysts, promoting the proton-coupled electron transfer process, achieving high performance reduction of CO2 to CH4.

Metal-free carbon-based catalysts offered a promising alternative for efficient electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction (ECO2RR), presenting potential advantages over transition metal-based counterparts. However, challenges such as low Faraday efficiencies and limited current densities persist. Herein, we synthesized CQDs with high Lewis basic sites to enhance CO2 adsorption and activation, resulting in an optimized CQD-2 catalyst that achieved a Faradaic efficiency of 52.00 % and a current density of 178 mA cm−2 for CH4 production. In situ characterizations unveiled that CO2 was activated into *COOH intermediates, which further converted to *CO species. Concurrently, activated H2O molecule ionized into protons, facilitating reaction kinetics. This research provides valuable insights into designing carbon-based catalysts with high Lewis base content, offering significant prospects for advancing high-performance ECO2RR technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive utilization of fossil fuels continues to engender a significant surge in carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions, exacerbating air pollution and the greenhouse effect, thereby posing a dire threat to the Earth’s ecosystem [1–5]. Urgent measures are imperative to address this escalating environmental challenge, with the conversion of CO2 into value-added chemicals emerging as a pivotal strategy. Electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (ECO2RR) represents a compelling avenue for converting CO2 into commercially valuable chemical feedstocks and fuels, offering a feasible pathway to mitigate the harmful effects of CO2 emissions [6–10]. Nonetheless, the complexity of reaction intermediates and the competing reactions underscore the need to enhance catalyst activity and selectivity [11]. To this end, a diverse array of methodologies has been devised to regulate the interfacial and surface architecture of catalysts [12–14]. Notably, surface modification by introducing Lewis basic sites stands out as an efficient and versatile approach. The existing literature has indicated that electron-rich Lewis basic sites act as potent electron donors in catalytic reactions, enabling CO2 molecule activation and promoting its cleavage while modulating the electronic structure and local site reactivity of the catalysts [15–17].

Among the various electrocatalysts, metal-free carbon-based electrocatalysts offer a plethora of advantages, including abundant availability, cost-effectiveness, favorable conductivity, corrosion resistance, structural tunability, and environmental compatibility [18,19]. These inherent attributes render carbon-based electrocatalysts highly promising candidates for introducing Lewis basic sites and accelerating their development for ECO2RR applications. Notably, Lewis basic-functionalized carbon materials, such as those with amino or nitrogen sites, induce charge redistribution within the carbon framework, thereby exhibiting remarkable catalytic activity through interactions with CO2 (a Lewis acid) [20–23]. This interaction facilitates the capture and selective regulation of reaction intermediates, significantly enhancing catalytic performance. Furthermore, the microenvironment characteristics of the catalyst surface significantly influence the gas-diffusion layer (GDL) kinetics [24–27]. Hydrophilic catalysts often experience water flooding, leading to diminished selectivity, reduced energy efficiency, and shortened catalyst lifespan [28–30]. Therefore, synthesizing hydrophobic carbon materials enriched with Lewis basic sites holds promise for achieving high-performance CO2 reduction.

Herein, we selected zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as model catalysts [31]. Unlike conventional carbon materials, CQDs offer abundant surface functionalization. Employing a molecular fusion approach, we synthesized CQDs abundant in Lewis basic sites for use in the ECO2RR. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and CO2 temperature-programmed desorption (CO2-TPD) analyses confirmed the successful integration of Lewis basic sites. The experimental results demonstrated that Lewis basic sites could act as electron donors, promoting CO2 activation and accelerating reaction kinetics, leading to highly efficient ECO2RR. By virtue of these merits, the proof-of-concept CQDs catalyst achieved excellent electrocatalysis performances for converting CO2 to CH4. Furthermore, in-situ characterization techniques elucidated the reaction mechanism. Our findings offer crucial insights into the rational design of hydrophobic catalysts featuring a high concentration of Lewis basic sites.

Pyrene (C16H10, >97.0 %) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry. Acetonitrile (CH3CN, 99.9 %), Potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥90.0 %), Potassium bicarbonate (KHCO3, ≥99.5 %), and Isopropanol (≥99.7 %) were purchased from GENERAL-REAGENT. Nitric acid (HNO3, 65.0～68.0 %) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nafion (5 wt%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

The 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was synthesized using nitration reaction. In brief, pyrene (10.00 g) was slowly added to concentrated HNO3 (250 mL) and refluxed at 82 °C for 24 h. Then it was washed with water to neutral, and dried to obtain 1,3,6-trinitropyrene.

CQDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method of molecular fusion. Initially, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.5 g) was dissolved in acetonitrile (50 mL). Subsequently, 1,2-dicyanobenzene (0.33 g) was added, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. The resulting precursor solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. The autoclave was then heated to 200 °C in an oven and maintained at this temperature for 12 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove byproducts, obtaining the CQDs-2 solution. The suspension was subsequently dried at 60 °C for further characterization. CQD-1, CQD-3, CQD-4, and CQD-5 were prepared in the same way, except that 1,2-dicyanobenzene was added to 0.21 g, 0.5 g, 0.75 g, 1.16 g, respectively.

The optical properties of CQDs were measured by HORIBA Duetta. The time-resolved transient PL spectra of all samples were characterized by HORIBA FluoroMax with a 375 nm laser. Ultraviolet–visible (UV) spectra were recorded using the Shimazu UV-2700 spectrophotometer. TEM and HRTEM images were examined using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, HT7700) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were measured by a Bruker Dimension ICON-XR. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by PANalytical AERIS. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were analyzed by Thermofisher IS50. XPS spectra were acquired utilizing a Thermo scientific K-Alpha. CO2-temperature programmed desorption (CO2-TPD) was characterized by ChemBET Pulsar-TPR/TPD automated chemisorption analyzer. Zeta potential of photocatalysts was measured by Malvern ZetasizerNano ZS nanoparticle size analyzer. The surface water contact angle of samples was measured using an Angle Contact Metering System (JCY-1). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) were characterized with Autolab (AUT88032) electrochemical workstation. Electrochemical measurements were performed using CHI760 electrochemical workstation. In situ diffuse infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy was meticulously recorded using a Thermo fisher, IS50. In situ Raman was obtained by XploRA PLUS (HORIBA FRANCE SAS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of catalysts and structural characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs catalysts was conducted to illustrate the role of Lewis basic sites in the ECO2RR via a molecular fusion reaction as depicted in Fig. 1 a. To gain comprehensive insights into the structural morphology of the synthesized materials, TEM and AFM were employed. TEM analysis (Fig. 1b, 1c and Fig. S1) revealed the presence of uniformly dispersed nanodots with average lateral sizes ranging from approximately 2.24 nm to 2.75 nm for CQD-1 to CQD-5, respectively. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1d showcased the high crystallinity structure of CQD-2 with well-defined lattice spacings of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [32]. AFM imaging delineated an average thickness of 1.0 nm for CQD-2, elucidating that it consisted of one or two layers of graphite (Fig. 1e and 1f) [33]. The luminescence characteristics of the synthesized CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 1g, S2 and S3, revealed distinct emission wavelengths at 580 and 635 nm, respectively. Furthermore, analysis of their emission lifetimes unveiled triple exponential lifetimes ranging from 2.39 to 3.27 ns (Fig. S4 and Table S1).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CQDs catalysts (Fig. 2 a) further exhibited the structural characteristics of the CQDs, with a prominent peak appearing at 25.5°, corresponding to the (002) graphite facets [34]. This observation was in accordance with the lattice spacing determined from HRTEM imaging. Raman spectra of all CQDs (Fig. S5) showed characteristic D and G peaks at ∼ 1348 cm−1 and ∼ 1594 cm−1, respectively, attributed to sp3 defects and sp2-hybridized carbon. The calculated ratio of ID/IG, serving as a proxy for defect density, ranged from about 0.36 to 0.67 from CQD-1 to CQD-5, consistent with the high crystallinity of the synthesized CODs.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to investigate the surface functional groups and chemical bonds of the CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. Notably, an intense peak at approximately 1707 cm−1 was identified, attributed to C=O group. The bending and stretching vibrations of aromatic C-H were observed at 870 and 3060 cm−1, while peaks at 1328 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 corresponded to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrational modes of NO2 group. Furthermore, the successful introduction of cyano groups (C≡N) was confirmed by a peak observed at 2233 cm−1, with CQD-2 showing a notably stronger C≡N signal than the other samples [35,36]. These findings confirmed the presence of nitrogen-containing surface functional groups in these optimized catalysts. Contact Angle tests revealed exemplary hydrophobicity across all CQDs (Fig. S6). The CQDs, with their superior hydrophobic properties, could effectively prevent CO2 absorption in highly alkaline electrolytes, establish a solid–liquid-gas three-phase interface, concentrate CO2, and suppress the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [37,38].

The electronic structures and surface valence states of the CQDs were probed using XPS. As depicted in Fig. S7 and Table S2, C 1s (285 eV), O 1s (532 eV), and N 1s (400 eV) peaks existed in the as-prepared CQDs. The deconvolution of the C 1s spectra (Fig. 2c and Table S3) revealed three primary peaks at ∼284.8, ∼266.3 and ∼288.4 eV, representing the carbon configurations in graphite (C-C/C=C) [39], carbon atoms of cyano groups (C≡N) [35], and carbon doubly bound to oxygen (C=O) [40], respectively. Additionally, the high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 2d and Table S4) unveiled distinctive signals attributed to nitrogen atoms in cyanogen bonds ∼399.6 eV (C≡N) [41], graphitic bonds ∼401.9 eV (C-N) and nitro bonds ∼405.7 eV (NO2) [42], indicating a heterogeneous composition. From the atom ratio of these peaks, the C≡N and NO2 species dominated in the N category (Fig. 2e and Table S5), endowing them with a negative charge. With CQDs as an ideal platform, the C≡N group, known for its strong Lewis basicity, was chosen as the proof-of-concept modifier [43]. This group served as an efficient electron donor to facilitate the sluggish CO2 dissociation process. Moreover, the O 1s spectra of CQDs exhibited peaks at ∼533.8, ∼532.9, and ∼531.7 eV, corresponding to O-H, NO2, and C=O functionalities, respectively (Fig. S8 and Table S6) [44–47].

The presence of C≡N, NO2, and C=O sites facilitated the exhibition of Lewis base properties, as further demonstrated by CO2-TPD measurements (Fig. S9). Notably, CQD-2 exhibited the highest Lewis base strength and density due to the presence of defective N or O sites, thus confirming that the interfacial synergies were the primary source of the catalytic activity. Collectively, these findings suggested that the synthesized CQDs, enriched with Lewis basic sites and exhibiting hydrophobicity, significantly facilitate CO2 adsorption on the active sites. This modification results in substantially enhanced ECO2RR performance compared to counterparts lacking the Lewis basic groups.

Following the deposition of CQDs catalysts onto a carbon-based GDL with a Nafion perfluorinated resin binder, the ECO2RR electrocatalytic performance was evaluated in a flow cell with 1 M KOH (flow rate: 5 mL min−1) and CO2 gas (flow rate: 15 mL min−1). The cell was equipped with an anion exchange membrane (AEM). In this setup, CO2 was reduced to methane at the cathode and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) occurred at the anode (Fig. 3 a). The gaseous products were quantified using online gas chromatography (GC). Fig. 3b shows the electrochemical reduction properties of CQD-2, with error bars representing the standard deviation from independent measurements. Over a range of applied potentials from −0.8 to −2.8 V vs RHE, CO product gradually decreased. Notably, the faradaic efficiency for methane (FE(CH4)) peaked at 52 % ± 1.36 % at −2.0 V before diminishing at more negative potentials, accompanied by the generation of a minor quantity of C2H4. This phenomenon could be attributed to the increasing prominence of the HER as a competitive reaction at more negative potentials.

Further insights into the primary products (CH4/H2) at −2.0 V were gleaned through comparative analysis across the CQD-1 to CQD-5 samples (Fig. 3c). This comparative assessment unequivocally confirmed the superior FE for CH4 production exhibited by CQD-2 relative to the other samples. Specifically, the FE for CH4 generation by CQD-2 surpassed that of CQD-5 by approximately 2.5-fold. Conversely, the FE(H2) exhibited an opposite trend, with CQD-2 displaying the lowest FE(H2) values. This observation indicated that CQD-2 was the least active toward the HER, further accentuating its prowess as an efficient catalyst for CO2 reduction.

The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) profile in Fig. 3d elucidated the electron transport capability. Notably, CQD-2 exhibited markedly higher current density under a CO2 atmosphere compared to other CQDs, indicating its superior electrochemical activity. Additionally, CQD-2-CO2 exhibited a higher current density than CQD-2-Ar (Fig. S10), further indicating a clear preference for ECO2RR over HER [48,49]. This observation was further corroborated by the partial current density of CH4 (j(CH4)) illustrated in Fig. 3e. CQD-2 demonstrated excellent activity in the 1 M KOH electrolyte, yielding a current density of 178 mA cm−2 at −2.4 V, significantly surpassing that of the other CQDs. This heightened performance could be attributed to the favorable influence of Lewis basic sites doping on the proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) process [50–52], the performance of CQD-2 surpasses that of most reported catalysts in the literature (Table S7). In addition to evaluating catalytic activity, the stability of CQD-2 was assessed at −2.0 V vs RHE in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte, demonstrating a slight decrease during the stability test (Fig. S11).

The kinetics of CH4 evolution was elucidated through Tafel slope analysis. As shown in Fig. 3f, CQD-2 exhibited a smaller Tafel slope (389 mV dec-1) compared to CQD-1, CQD-3, CQD-4, and CQD-5, indicating that a lower overpotential was required to achieve the same current density. This enhancement in CH4 conversion capability was likely associated with the increased reactivity of the C≡N Lewis basic sites on its surface, which may facilitate the kinetics of H2O protonation reactions and the subsequent generation of *COOH species. To further investigate the reaction kinetics, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves for all five materials were measured at varying scan rates (Figure S11). Notably, with increasing scan rates, CQD-2 exhibited the largest CV curve area, suggesting superior catalytic performance (Figs. S12). Moreover, the ECSA was a critical metric for evaluating the ECO2RR to CH4. The ECSA was calculated through double-layer capacitance (Cdl) (Figs. S13 and 3g) [53], revealing that CQD-2 possesses the largest ECSA (0.21 cm2). This increase in ECSA may be attributed to the exposure of more Lewis basic active sites, which enhanced CO2′s ability to accept electrons and bound with active centers to form intermediates, thereby promoting the kinetics of CO2 reduction. These analyses indicated that the rate-determining step may involve a proton-coupled electron transfer process.

Zeta potential measurements provided further insights into the surface charge characteristics of the CQDs (Fig. 3h and Table S8). Compared with CQD-1 (−47.93 mV), CQD-3 (−38.13 mV), CQD-4 (−48.87 mV), and CQD-5 (−51.3 mV), the Zeta potential of CQD-2 significantly increased to −36.37 mV, attributed to the increase of Lewis basic sites. This phenomenon enhanced local alkalinity and regulated the local electric field [54]. Finally, EIS was performed to investigate the charge transfer kinetics in Fig. 3i. CQD-2 exhibited an apparent lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 17.8 Ω, indicating the superiority of Lewis basic sites in promoting CO2 reduction process kinetics at electrolyte/electrode interface. Therefore, the incorporation of Lewis basic sites was beneficial in reducing charge transfer resistance and enhancing charge transfer ability of the CQD-2 catalyst.

To gain deeper insights into the reaction mechanism for CH4 production during ECO2RR, in situ attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (in situ ATR-FTIR) measurements and in situ Raman were performed for the CQD-2 electrocatalyst to identify key adsorbed intermediates via electrocatalysis (Figs. S14 and S15). The ATR-FTIR spectra of the catalysts were collected at −2.0 V vs RHE over time. As depicted in Fig. 4 a and S16, the peak observed at 3350 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibrations, while that at 1640 cm−1 indicated the bending of adsorbed H2O molecule [55]. As time increased, the H2O molecule peak intensity gradually decreased, indicating the proton formation on the CQD-2 electrocatalyst. Absorption bands at 1270 and 1386 cm−1 were ascribed to the *COOH intermediate [56], widely recognized as pivotal for CO2 activation, CO conversion and further reduction. Notably, the absorption bands at 1490, 1225, and 1157 cm−1 were assigned to the *CH2O, *CHO, and *CH3O, respectively [55,56], representing key intermediates for CH4 formation. Furthermore, the absorption bands at 1186 cm−1 could be assigned to *OCCOH for the binding behavior of C–C coupling, corresponding to the production of C2H4 [57]. However, no significant peaks appeared in the Ar atmosphere (Fig. 4d), indicating that CH4 was derived from the conversion of CO2 rather than the self-decomposition of the CQD-2 catalyst. In addition, key reaction intermediates were also traced by in situ Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 4c). The peaks observed at 1658 cm−1 were attributed to adsorbed H2O [58]. The additional peaks at 1897 and 2009 cm−1 obviously observed, corresponded to adsorbed *CO intermediates in bridge (*CObridge) and atop configuration (*COatop) on CQD-2 [59,60]. As time increased, the peak areas increased gradually, demonstrating that *COOH was further reduced to *CO, promoting the formation of CH4. The presence of abundant intermediate species confirmed the effective electrochemical reduction of CO2 by the CQD-2 electrocatalyst.

Building upon the in-depth experimental findings from in situ ATR-FTIR and Raman, a plausible CO2 reduction to CH4 route for the CQD-2 electrocatalyst was proposed in Fig. 4d. Initially, as a Lewis acid, CO2 and H2O molecules accepted electron attack and were adsorbed by the Lewis base active centers of the catalyst. The H2O molecule was activated to ionize into protons at the Lewis basic sites. Then, the adsorbed CO2 molecule was activated to *COOH by electrons and protons, followed by dehydration to form *CO. Most importantly, the adsorbed *CO underwent further electrons and protons transfers to generate *CHO, *CH2O, and *CH3O in succession, eventually leading to CH4 production. It can be reasonably inferred that the formation of CH4 likely occurred through the proton–electron transfer route, which is responsible for CQD-2 catalyst’s excellent selectivity towards CH4 products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding the correlation between physicochemical properties and electrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: Heatmaps generated from the Pearson correlation coefficient were employed to gain a deeper understanding of the pivotal role of Lewis basic sites in boosting reaction kinetics for CO2RR, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a and 5b. The color spectra of the heatmap reflect the strength and direction of the correlation: red squares signify positive correlation (correlation coefficient near 1), white squares denote negligible correlation (correlation coefficient near 0), and blue squares indicate negative correlation (correlation coefficient close to −1). The analysis unveiled that CH4 conversion and current density were positively correlated with O 1s/C 1s ratio and N content, indicating that oxygen- and nitrogen-containing Lewis basic functional groups could promote the selectivity and activity of the reaction. Conversely, no significant correlation was observed between CH4 selectivity/current density and C-N content, suggesting the inert nature of C-N as an active site for CH4 production. The content of nitro and cyano groups exhibited positive correlation with CH4 selectivity and current density, attributed to their role as Lewis basic sites that could provide electron pairs to promote the reaction. Notably, the cyano group exhibited a more pronounced effect likely due to the increased lone-pair electron density, which facilitated the binding of CO2 molecule.

Further investigation into the physical structure revealed negative correlations between average particle size and ID/IG ratio from Raman spectroscopy with CH4 conversion and current density, attributing this to smaller CQDs with higher graphitization possessing more active sites, which were advantageous for CH4 conversion. Additionally, positive correlations were observed between zeta potential, ECSA, and CH4 conversion/current density, indicating that Zeta potential modulated local electric fields and inhibited HER, while larger ECSA was more conducive to the conversion of CH4 intermediates and stabilization. Moreover, impedance and surface hydrophobicity emerged as critical parameters affecting ECO2RR performance. Multivariate correlation analysis synthesized these findings, emphasizing the significance of increasing Lewis basic sites to effectively improve the reactivity while adjusting surface properties to inhibit unwanted reactions. The synergistic interplay of various properties, including graphitization degree, Lewis basic sites content, electronegativity, and ECSA synergistically determined the activity of CQDs catalysts, offering invaluable insights for designing ECO2RR catalysts enriched in Lewis basic sites.

In summary, this study presents a molecular fusion solvothermal approach for synthesizing hydrophobic CQDs catalysts enriched with Lewis basic sites. The CQD-2 catalyst exhibited optimal kinetic performance in ECO2RR, achieving the highest CH4 conversion (FE of 52.00 % ± 1.36 %) and a partial current density of 178.00 mA cm−2. Combining in situ characterization with multivariate correlation analysis, it was demonstrated that the modification of Lewis basic sites altered the interfacial microenvironment, establishing a solid–liquid-gas three-phase interface and a high-concentration CO2 layer. Moreover, Lewis basic active sites activated water molecules on the catalyst surface to form protons, thereby facilitating CO2 adsorption and subsequent reactions. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the microscopic mechanisms and provide new perspectives for the design of next-generation catalysts.

Shuai Fu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Bijun Tang: Investigation. Zeming Wang: Investigation. Guangbin An: Investigation. Mingwan Zhang: Investigation. Kang Wang: Investigation. Wenhui Liu: Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Investigation. Baohua Zhang: Investigation. Liang Wang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022) and Changsha Natural Science Foundation (kq2208392). This work is Supported by Shanghai Technical Service Center of Science and Engineering Computing, Shanghai University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.157207.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ exsolved ultrafine Ni nanoparticles from CeZrNiO2 solid solution for efficient photothermal catalytic CO2 reduction by CH4

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Ji, L. Ji, S. Wu, L. Meng, Y. Jia, Z. Liu, S. Dong, J. Tian, Y. Li, In-situ exsolved ultrafine Ni nanoparticles from CeZrNiO2 solid solution for efficient photothermal catalytic CO2 reduction by CH4, Adv. sb:title.sb:maintitle: CO2-to-methane electroreduction gets a helping hand

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic Liquid-Induced Product Switching in CO2 Electroreduction on Copper Reaction Interface
